text: Let's move from an over-engineered approach to an under-engineered one. (regarding C++ range handling). I think algorithms should be matched to use cases.
The comment there is not about [-3, 17] being an obscure output range from a distribution. It is that the distribution must be able to handle a random generator that outputs an unusual range.
The modulo approach is biased for all ranges which don't divide the full range.
In TXR Lisp, the algorithm finds the tightest power-of-two bounding box for the modulus, clip the pseudo-random number to that power-of-two range, and then reject if outside the target.
for a randomly chosen range of size n, you reject a throw with probability 1/4, right?
probability ends up being 1-ln(2) if you think of n as being uniformly random
For any power of two m, for any range size n (m/2 < n <= m), rejection probability is (m-n)/m. Corrected calculation.
n uniformly random from what distribution?
Note that this article is not really about RNGs themselves, but mostly how to use one to generate an unbiased number within a given range from a raw RNG output.
The article's conclusion was that the PRNG generation method used is usually not the bottleneck, but how you take that to get a result is.
I wonder if the Bitmask with Rejection method would be more efficient if you sometimes made the mask one bit larger than strictly necessary.
It seems crazy to me that there's no way to produce unbiased numbers in an arbitrary range without rejection sampling and a loop. Is there a proof of this?
you need a loop if you don't control the underlying generator
You don't need rejection sampling, but you do need a loop. A finite number of samples is not sufficient.
Proposition: There is no way, given iid uniform samples in [0,n), to always construct a uniform sample in [0,m) with a bounded number of samples, when m does not divide a power of n.
Personally I'm a fan of the xoshiro generator.
some generators have weak low-order bits. For example, the Xoroshiro+ generator has weak low order bits, which matters when modulo passes those bits directly into the result.
